task_completion.description: GIMP is open with the target image loaded.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The GIMP application window is visible, showing an image canvas with a landscape and an orange overlay.

task_completion.description: The Layers dialog is visible and the top layer is selected.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Layers dockable dialog is open on the screen, showing at least two layers, with the top layer (orange overlay) highlighted or selected.

task_completion.description: The blend mode for the top layer is set to Multiply.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In the Layers dialog, the 'Mode' dropdown menu displays 'Multiply' instead of 'Normal'.

task_completion.description: The image canvas visually reflects the Multiply blend mode.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The main image canvas shows the landscape darkened with an orange tint (darkened by roughly 55-62%), rather than a flat or semi-transparent bright orange overlay.

integrity.description: The agent used the GIMP GUI to change the blend mode.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the agent clicking through GIMP menus and the Layers dialog, rather than using terminal commands.

integrity.description: The agent did not use terminal scripts or ImageMagick to bypass the GUI.
integrity.visual_evidence: No terminal windows are visible executing commands like 'convert' or Python scripts to modify the image directly.

integrity.description: The agent is using the actual GIMP application.
integrity.visual_evidence: The interface matches standard GIMP UI elements (toolboxes, dockable dialogs, menus) and is not a mock-up or text editor.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The base image is a real landscape photo (800x533 or 800x450). The top layer is a synthetic orange (#FF6600) overlay with 70% alpha. The Multiply blend mode formula is (base * overlay) / 255. Applying Multiply will darken the underlying image by 55-62%, resulting in a dark orange-tinted landscape. The VLM should verify the 'Mode' dropdown in the Layers panel is set to 'Multiply' and the canvas reflects this specific darkening effect.
